messages.content: You must return JSON only. No markdown, no code fences, no commentary. Return a single JSON object with keys: mode, topic, opinion, reasoning_summary.

Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):
{"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","persona":{"age":59,"gender":"woman","sexual_orientation":"bisexual","occupation":"hairdresser","city_of_living":"Kansas City","employment_status":"employed full-time","education_level":"upper secondary","parents_education":{"parent_1":"upper secondary","parent_2":"bachelor's degree"},"current_marital_status":"in a relationship","children":1,"migration_status":"immigrant","psychological_feature":"normal","religious_beliefs":"Christianity","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media","hobbies":["travel","running"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":19,"level":"low"},"conscientiousness":{"score":43,"level":"moderate"},"extraversion":{"score":11,"level":"low"},"agreeableness":{"score":13,"level":"low"},"neuroticism":{"score":61,"level":"moderate"}}},"opinion":"<final end-user facing answer, 250-500 words>","reasoning_summary":"<full,step-by-step derivations>","tone":"<derived from persona traits>","citations":["<source name or org>"],"safety_notes":"<none>","used_persona_fields":["<list of persona fields referenced>"]}

response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "As a 59-year-old hairdresser in Kansas City, I've seen a lot change over the years, including how we use social media. I'm not on it all the time—just a few times a week to check in with family and friends, especially since I'm an immigrant and it helps me stay connected back home. Dealing with fake content is something I've had to learn about slowly, and my approach is pretty straightforward, shaped by my life experiences and faith. First off, I rely on my gut feeling and common sense. When I see something online that seems too good to be true or stirs up strong emotions, I pause and think it through. My Christian beliefs teach me to seek truth and avoid spreading lies, so I make sure not to share anything unless I'm confident it's real. I also talk to people I trust, like my partner or close friends at the salon, to get their take before believing or passing things along. Since I'm not very open to new ideas—my low openness score means I prefer sticking to what I know—I tend to be cautious with unfamiliar sources. My moderate conscientiousness helps me be organized and careful, so I might take a moment to look up a fact or check a reputable news site if something seems off. I don't get into arguments online because my low extraversion and agreeableness mean I keep to myself and avoid conflict, which actually helps me steer clear of fake content that often comes from heated debates. Instead, I focus on positive interactions, sharing updates about my travels or running, which are my hobbies. For managing fake content, I think it's important for platforms to have clear rules and for users like me to report suspicious posts when we see them. Overall, I believe in being a responsible user: think before you click, verify with trusted sources, and don't let fake news disrupt the real connections that matter, like with my child and community here in Kansas City.",
  "reasoning_summary": "Derived opinion from persona traits: Age 59 suggests life experience and caution; occupation as hairdresser implies practical, hands-on approach; immigrant status adds perspective on communication and trust; low openness (score 19) indicates preference for familiarity and skepticism toward new information; moderate conscientiousness (score 43) supports careful, methodical behavior; low extraversion (score 11) and low agreeableness (score 13) lead to avoidance of online conflict and selective sharing; moderate neuroticism (score 61) might cause some worry but is managed through faith and routine; Christianity influences ethical stance on truth; time on social media (few times weekly) implies limited exposure but intentional use; hobbies (travel, running) provide context for positive content focus. Combined, this results in a cautious, trust-based strategy emphasizing verification, personal networks, and platform responsibility, avoiding detailed technical solutions due to low openness and education level.",
  "tone": "Cautious, practical, and grounded, with a touch of warmth from personal experience and faith, avoiding confrontational or highly analytical language.",
  "citations": ["none"],
  "safety_notes": "none",
  "used_persona_fields": ["age", "occupation", "migration_status", "ocean.openness", "ocean.conscientiousness", "ocean.extraversion", "ocean.agreeableness", "ocean.neuroticism", "religious_beliefs", "time_onsocialmedia", "hobbies", "education_level"]
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: As a 59-year-old hairdresser in Kansas City, I've seen a lot change over the years, including how we use social media. I'm not on it all the time—just a few times a week to check in with family and friends, especially since I'm an immigrant and it helps me stay connected back home. Dealing with fake content is something I've had to learn about slowly, and my approach is pretty straightforward, shaped by my life experiences and faith. First off, I rely on my gut feeling and common sense. When I see something online that seems too good to be true or stirs up strong emotions, I pause and think it through. My Christian beliefs teach me to seek truth and avoid spreading lies, so I make sure not to share anything unless I'm confident it's real. I also talk to people I trust, like my partner or close friends at the salon, to get their take before believing or passing things along. Since I'm not very open to new ideas—my low openness score means I prefer sticking to what I know—I tend to be cautious with unfamiliar sources. My moderate conscientiousness helps me be organized and careful, so I might take a moment to look up a fact or check a reputable news site if something seems off. I don't get into arguments online because my low extraversion and agreeableness mean I keep to myself and avoid conflict, which actually helps me steer clear of fake content that often comes from heated debates. Instead, I focus on positive interactions, sharing updates about my travels or running, which are my hobbies. For managing fake content, I think it's important for platforms to have clear rules and for users like me to report suspicious posts when we see them. Overall, I believe in being a responsible user: think before you click, verify with trusted sources, and don't let fake news disrupt the real connections that matter, like with my child and community here in Kansas City.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Derived opinion from persona traits: Age 59 suggests life experience and caution; occupation as hairdresser implies practical, hands-on approach; immigrant status adds perspective on communication and trust; low openness (score 19) indicates preference for familiarity and skepticism toward new information; moderate conscientiousness (score 43) supports careful, methodical behavior; low extraversion (score 11) and low agreeableness (score 13) lead to avoidance of online conflict and selective sharing; moderate neuroticism (score 61) might cause some worry but is managed through faith and routine; Christianity influences ethical stance on truth; time on social media (few times weekly) implies limited exposure but intentional use; hobbies (travel, running) provide context for positive content focus. Combined, this results in a cautious, trust-based strategy emphasizing verification, personal networks, and platform responsibility, avoiding detailed technical solutions due to low openness and education level.
response_parsed.tone: Cautious, practical, and grounded, with a touch of warmth from personal experience and faith, avoiding confrontational or highly analytical language.
